page.text:
However, theories of unrestricted comprehension are fundamentally unsound.
Russell's Paradox is a well known example of an inconsistency which arises in
such a theory. Namely, if unrestricted comprehension is assumed, one could
consider the set $A = \{x \mid x \notin x\}$, that is, the set of all sets which
do not contain themselves. Asking whether $A \in A$ leads to a contradiction:
if $A \in A$, then by the definition of $A$ we conclude $A \notin A$, and if
$A \notin A$, then again by the definition of $A$ we conclude $A \in A$. One is
left with the highly problematic statement that $A \in A \iff A \notin A$.
Thus, this development proceeds with an axiomatic characterization of sets which
more carefully characterizes them and allows one to avoid such paradoxes.

"
However, theories of unrestricted comprehension are fundamentally unsound.
Russell's Paradox is a well known example of an inconsistency which arises in
